instance InternalTempSensorAvg1 : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Digital temperature sensor placed inside the incubator box, used for averaging internal air temperature (sensor 1)."

]

instance InternalTempSensorAvg2 : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Digital temperature sensor placed inside the incubator box, used for averaging internal air temperature (sensor 2)."
    DTDFVocab:producedData InternalTempDataAvg2
]

]

// C4: Physical-to-virtual interaction (Data transmitted)
instance InternalTempDataAvg1 : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [

instance InternalTempDataAvg2 : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom InternalTempSensorAvg2
]

instance InternalTempDataHotSpot : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [

]

]

// C10: Data
instance TemperatureDataInternalAvg1 : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Timestamped temperature reading (°C) from internal average sensor 1, published via RabbitMQ."
    DTDFVocab:fromData InternalTempDataAvg1
    DTDFVocab:inputTo RabbitMQBrokerEnabler
]

instance TemperatureDataInternalAvg2 : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Timestamped temperature reading (°C) from internal average sensor 2, published via RabbitMQ."
    DTDFVocab:fromData InternalTempDataAvg2
    DTDFVocab:inputTo RabbitMQBrokerEnabler
]

instance TemperatureDataInternalHotSpot : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Timestamped temperature reading (°C) from internal hotspot sensor, published via RabbitMQ."
    DTDFVocab:fromData InternalTempDataHotSpot
    DTDFVocab:inputTo RabbitMQBrokerEnabler
]

instance TemperatureDataExternal : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Timestamped ambient temperature reading (°C) from external sensor, published via RabbitMQ."
    DTDFVocab:fromData ExternalTempData
    DTDFVocab:inputTo RabbitMQBrokerEnabler
]

// C11: Enablers (message broker & processing engines)
instance RabbitMQBrokerEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "RabbitMQ AMQP broker that routes sensor measurements and controller commands within the incubator Digital Twin."
    DTDFVocab:enables CalibrationService,
                     MonitoringService,
                     PredictionSimulationService,
                     VisualizationService,
                     AnomalyDetectionService,
                     WhatIfSimulationService,
                     SelfAdaptationLoopService
]

instance CalibrationEngineEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Calibration engine that consumes temperature data to adjust parameters of Model A and Model B."
    DTDFVocab:enables CalibrationService
]

instance MonitoringEngineEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Monitoring engine that continuously compares measured temperature against lower/upper limits (LL, UL)."
    DTDFVocab:enables MonitoringService
]

instance PredictionEngineEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Prediction engine that runs calibrated thermal models to forecast incubator temperature dynamics."
    DTDFVocab:enables PredictionSimulationService
]

instance VisualizationEngineEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Visualization engine that stores incoming data in a time‑series database and renders real‑time dashboards."
    DTDFVocab:enables VisualizationService
]

instance KalmanFilterEngineEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Kalman filter engine that fuses Model B with sensor data to detect anomalies and estimate non‑measured heat‑bed temperature."
    DTDFVocab:enables AnomalyDetectionService
]

instance CoSimulationEngineEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Co‑simulation engine that performs what‑if studies using historic or live Physical Twin data."
    DTDFVocab:enables WhatIfSimulationService
]

instance AdaptiveControlEngineEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Adaptive control engine that executes the eight‑step self‑adaptation loop to re‑configure the bang‑bang controller."
    DTDFVocab:enables SelfAdaptationLoopService
]

// C6: Services
instance CalibrationService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "On‑demand calibration service that automatically adjusts parameters of Model A and Model B using streamed temperature data."
    DTDFVocab:provides CalibrationInsight
]

]

]

]

]

]

]

]

instance PhysicalIncubator : DTDFVocab:Component [
    base:desc "Thermal incubator CPS consisting of insulated styrofoam box, heat‑bed, fan, temperature sensors, Raspberry Pi controller, low‑level driver, and RabbitMQ communication server."
]

instance virtual_to_physical_interaction : DTDFVocab:VirtualToPhysical [
    base:desc "The Digital Twin generates control commands that are sent as RabbitMQ messages to the controller running on the Raspberry Pi. Command types include setting the lower and upper temperature limits (LL, UL), issuing heating commands (turning the heatbed on for a duration H), and waiting commands (duration C) that implement a bang‑bang control strategy. Each command is encapsulated in an AMQP message format understood by the controller. The controller validates the command against the safe protocol embedded in the low‑level driver, executes the action on the heatbed or fan, and publishes a state‑update message back to the RabbitMQ server as confirmation of execution. Feedback loops are realised by the controller sharing its current control state (e.g., heating on/off, current temperature limits) through the same broker."
]

instance twinning_time_scale : DTDFVocab:TimeScale [
    base:desc "The low‑level driver periodically reads temperature sensor values and publishes them to a RabbitMQ message broker; the controller also subscribes to the same broker for commands and state updates. Synchronisation between the physical twin and the digital components is thus achieved through asynchronous message passing over RabbitMQ, but the documents do not specify exact sampling frequencies, latency bounds, or temporal granularity."
]

instance dt_constellation : DTDFVocab:Constellation [
    base:desc "The digital-twin constellation consists of a Raspberry Pi‑based controller, a low-level driver, a RabbitMQ message broker, calibration algorithms that update the thermal models, and monitor components that evaluate safety and optimality; coordination is realized through asynchronous message passing via RabbitMQ, with calibration results feeding the models and monitors, and human operators intervening when violations are detected."
]

instance fidelity_and_validity_considerations : DTDFVocab:FidelityConsideration [
    base:desc "Model fidelity rests on four explicit assumptions: (1) uniform temperature distribution inside the box (ensured by the internal fan); (2) negligible heat accumulation in the box walls; (3) constant specific heat capacity of the air; (4) all electrical power is transferred to heat the air (relaxed in Model B). Model A adheres to all assumptions, while Model B omits Assumption 4 to better capture the heatbed's thermal inertia. Validation is performed by comparing model predictions with experimentally recorded temperature trajectories; Figure 13 demonstrates that Model B provides a closer match to observed system magnitude and time scale. Calibration (parameter estimation) is carried out automatically using a non‑linear optimisation routine that fits the model to the sensor data, thereby quantifying and reducing model error."
]

instance dt_technical_connection : DTDFVocab:TechnicalConnection [
    base:desc "All components communicate via a RabbitMQ message broker (AMQP over TCP/IP). The network topology is a star configuration where the RabbitMQ server acts as the central hub; the Raspberry Pi (running the low‑level driver and controller) and any DT components (calibration module, anomaly detector, visualisation dashboard) connect as clients. RabbitMQ provides persistence, delivery acknowledgements and automatic reconnection, contributing to reliability and redundancy. Security mechanisms such as TLS encryption or authentication are not detailed in the provided documents. Bandwidth and latency requirements are not quantified, but the system is described as lightweight and suitable for real‑time interaction."
]

instance dt_hosting_deployment : DTDFVocab:Deployment [
    base:desc "The implementation is deployed on‑premise: the physical twin runs on a Raspberry Pi, and the RabbitMQ server is hosted locally (or on a reachable on‑premise machine). DT services such as calibration, Kalman‑filter based state estimation, and visualisation run on the same edge hardware or on a connected workstation; data are stored in a time‑series database that can be accessed by a dashboard. The documents do not specify cloud or edge hosting beyond the Raspberry Pi, nor do they give precise computational or storage sizing, scalability strategies, or high‑availability configurations."
]

instance horizontal_integration : DTDFVocab:HoriIntegration [
    base:desc "External integration is performed through the RabbitMQ message broker, which serves as the protocol for data exchange between the DT components and any external services. The hardware and software artefacts are released as open‑source code in a public repository, enabling reuse in other projects, but no additional interoperability standards or connections to other enterprise systems are described."
]

instance security_and_safety_considerations : DTDFVocab:SecuritySafety [
    base:desc "The incubator DT incorporates safety mechanisms at both the cyber and physical layers. The low‑level driver embeds a safe protocol that continuously monitors temperature readings and enforces a safe state for the physical twin, preventing unsafe actuation. The controller implements a bang‑bang control strategy with explicit temperature limits (Lower Limit LL and Upper Limit UL) and defined heating (H) and waiting (C) durations, ensuring the heat‑bed is not over‑driven. Communication between the controller, low‑level driver and the digital twin is mediated by a RabbitMQ message broker, which provides reliable message delivery for safety‑critical commands. Anomaly detection and heat‑bed state estimation are performed by a Kalman filter that processes Model B predictions together with real‑time sensor data, enabling early detection of deviations from expected thermal behavior. These combined measures constitute the security and safety considerations described in the documents."
]
